meta.title: @Input/@Output vs shared service for Angular component communication
meta.summary: An Angular page has a parent container and a few child UI components that need to send user actions upward and receive current state downward. Would you use @Input/@Output or a shared service?

scenario: You are building an Angular orders page. `OrdersPageComponent` owns the current filters, selected sort, and the loaded result set. Under it, there is a `FilterBarComponent`, a `SortMenuComponent`, and a `ResultsToolbarComponent`. Those children need to show the current values and emit user actions such as 'status changed', 'sort changed', and 'clear filters'. One teammate wants to wire the page with `@Input()` and `@Output()` because the parent already owns the state. Another teammate wants to create a shared service immediately so components can communicate through that instead of bubbling events through the parent. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Use @Input() and @Output()
options.summary: Fits best when the data flow already matches the component tree and the parent is the natural owner of the state.
whenItWins: A parent container clearly owns the state and child components mostly need to render inputs and emit user intent back up.
The communication path is short and understandable, so explicit bindings make dependencies easier to trace.
You want future developers to see data flow directly in the template instead of hunting through an extra service layer.
watchOutFor: If communication starts crossing distant branches of the tree or several components need long-lived shared state, binding everything through parents can get noisy.
A weak answer uses inputs and outputs by habit even when the feature no longer fits a simple parent-owned flow.

options.label: Use a shared service
options.summary: Fits best when several components need a shared feature state or coordination that no single parent cleanly owns.
whenItWins: Siblings or distant components need to share state over time, and lifting everything to a parent would create awkward plumbing.
The feature needs shared streams, cached values, or coordination that survives component replacement inside the route.
A single service boundary makes the feature easier to reason about than passing data through several wrapper components.
watchOutFor: A service can become an unnecessary event bus if the parent already owns the state and children only need standard input and output bindings.
A weak answer reaches for a service because it feels more scalable, without checking whether it is solving a real communication problem yet.

decisionMatrix.title: Clear parent-owned component tree
decisionMatrix.prompt: One parent owns the state and a few children only need to receive values and emit user actions.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the component tree already matches the data-flow boundary.
Still possible, but now you should explain why another coordination layer is helping more than explicit bindings.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: The page container already owns filters and sort state, while children mostly render and emit user intent.

cells.note: This is the cleanest answer because the parent is already the source of truth and the child interactions map neatly to outputs.
Harder to defend because the service would mostly duplicate ownership the parent already has.

decisionMatrix.title: State shared across several branches
decisionMatrix.prompt: Several components across the route need the same feature state, and no single parent cleanly owns the coordination anymore.

cells.note: You can still wire it, but the bindings may become plumbing rather than useful explicit design.
This becomes easier to defend when the feature needs a shared state boundary rather than just parent-child events.

evaluationDimensions.title: Who really owns the state?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. If one parent already owns the data, that usually changes the answer a lot.

evaluationDimensions.title: Does the communication match the component tree?
evaluationDimensions.description: Inputs and outputs are strongest when the data naturally moves down and user intent naturally moves up through the same tree.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is this just communication or real shared feature state?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer separates simple component communication from a broader feature state that several components need to coordinate around.

evaluationDimensions.title: Where will the next developer understand the flow faster?
evaluationDimensions.description: Explicit template bindings can be easier to trace than a service, but once bindings become plumbing, a service boundary may read better.

evaluationDimensions.title: Are you adding a service before the feature needs one?
evaluationDimensions.description: Junior developers often assume a service is more scalable by default. A stronger answer checks whether the extra layer solves a current problem.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean @Input/@Output because the parent already owns the page state
strongAnswer.summary: This orders page already has a natural owner for filters, sort, and results. The child components mostly display current values and emit user intent. That makes `@Input()` and `@Output()` the easier answer to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: The key signal is ownership. `OrdersPageComponent` is already the source of truth, so the simplest design is to pass state down and emit events back up.
That keeps dependencies explicit in the template. A future developer can see which child receives which value and which event the parent handles.
I would move toward a shared service only when the feature stops fitting a clear parent-owned tree, such as when several distant components need the same shared state or coordination over time.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would keep the communication with `@Input()` and `@Output()`. I would introduce a shared service later only if the feature grows into broader shared state that no single parent cleanly owns.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not use a service now so it scales better later?
interviewerPushback.answer: I would not add that layer until the feature actually needs it. In this prompt, the parent already owns the state, so a service would mostly duplicate that ownership and hide a flow that is currently easy to read.

interviewerPushback.question: When would a shared service clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When several siblings or distant components need shared feature state over time and pushing everything through parent bindings starts to look like plumbing instead of useful explicit flow.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If filters, selection, and other state started being shared across several route components with no clean single owner, I would move toward a shared feature service.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use a shared service because that is more scalable.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds generic and ignores the fact that the parent in this prompt already owns the state clearly.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use inputs and outputs because the parent can pass values down and get events back.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a sharper explanation of ownership and when a shared service becomes justified.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would use `@Input()` and `@Output()` because the page component already owns the filter and sort state. The child components mainly need to render those values and emit user actions such as changing a filter or clearing it. That keeps the flow explicit in the template. I would only move to a shared service if the feature grew into shared state across several component branches where no single parent cleanly owned the coordination anymore.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from state ownership, explains why explicit bindings fit the current tree, and still gives a clear boundary for when a service becomes the better tool.

answerFramework: Start by asking who owns the state in this prompt.
Check whether the communication path already matches the component tree.
Choose the lighter or broader coordination model based on that boundary.
Explain the main cost of the other option, either noisy plumbing or premature abstraction.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: inputs and outputs for clear parent-child flow, shared service for broader feature coordination.
antiPatterns: Choosing a shared service just because it feels more scalable.
Using inputs and outputs even after the feature clearly stopped fitting a parent-owned tree.
Treating simple component communication and shared feature state as if they were the same problem.
Talking only about boilerplate instead of talking about ownership and readability.
